allusions.surface: On the Nones of June the moon stood in the sun's path, and night
allusions.suspected_source: Ennius, Annales (Nonis Iunis soli luna obstitit et nox)
allusions.translator_note: Verbatim Ennian hexameter, cited as such.

allusions.surface: never less alone than when he is alone
allusions.translator_note: Cicero attributes the saying to his grandfather Africanus, on Cato's report.

allusions.surface: the eminently sensible man, the shrewd Aelius Sextus
allusions.suspected_source: Ennius, Annales (Egregie cordatus homo, catus Aelius Sextus)

allusions.surface: What watching is in heaven among the astrologers' signs
allusions.translator_note: Lyric passage from Ennius's tragedy Iphigenia, quoted at length.

allusions.surface: wishes to philosophize, but in a few things
no holy partnership of a kingdom is there, nor any faith
allusions.suspected_source: Ennius, Annales (nulla regni sancta societas nec fides est)

allusions.surface: Aratus, who, beginning to speak of great matters, thinks one must begin from Jupiter
allusions.translator_note: Cicero himself translated Aratus into Latin verse as a young man (the Aratea).

allusions.surface: as Homer says, with a nod turns the whole of Olympus
allusions.translator_note: Cicero referencing the famous nod of Zeus in the Iliad.

allusions.surface: their hearts long while are held by yearning, after the death of the best king
allusions.translator_note: Verbatim Ennian fragment, cited as such.

allusions.surface: O Romulus, divine Romulus, what guardian of his country the gods made you
allusions.translator_note: Continuation of the same Ennian passage on Romulus's apotheosis.

allusions.surface: When the unfillable throats of the people have parched with the thirst of liberty
allusions.translator_note: Cicero is openly translating from Plato's Republic on the cycle from democracy to tyranny.

allusions.surface: whom no man could surpass with iron or with gold
allusions.translator_note: Ennian hexameter on M. Curius Dentatus.

allusions.surface: borne in a chariot of winged serpents
allusions.translator_note: Pacuvian tragic line; the source play is uncertain.

allusions.surface: true law is right reason agreeing with nature
allusions.translator_note: Cicero adapts Stoic natural-law doctrine, perhaps with Posidonian intermediaries.

allusions.surface: On ancient morals stands the Roman state, and on its men
allusions.suspected_source: Ennius, Annales (moribus antiquis res stat Romana virisque)
allusions.translator_note: Famous Ennian hexameter, the opening of Book 5; preserved through Augustine.
The dream-vision of Homer at the opening of Ennius's Annales: Cicero's framing for the Somnium.

allusions.surface: what is always moved is eternal
allusions.translator_note: Cicero closely paraphrases the famous Phaedrus argument for the immortality of the self-moving soul.
